cookieBanner.title: Help us improve with privacy-first analytics
cookieBanner.description: We use PostHog to understand which features help most. Accept to enable full analytics with cookies. Reject to stay in cookieless mode where only anonymous, privacy-preserving measurement is used.

seo.title: Boerenbridge score tracking and rules | Game Scoreboard Manager
seo.description: Learn Boerenbridge (Oh Hell) rules, understand scoring, and track every round with a clear online scorecard.

howToPlayBoerenbridge.title: How to play Oh Hell
howToPlayBoerenbridge.subtitle: A classic trick-taking card game where predicting your wins matters more than winning itself.

overview.title: Game overview
overview.goalDescription: Predict exactly how many tricks you will win each round.
overview.roundsDescription: Cards go up from 1 to max (played twice), then back down.

howToPlay.title: How to play
howToPlay.step1Description: Each round, the dealer gives every player a number of cards equal to the round number. Round 1 = 1 card, Round 2 = 2 cards, and so on.
howToPlay.step2Description: After dealing, flip the top card of the remaining deck face-up. That suit is trump for this round. Trump cards beat any other suit. Exception: when playing the maximum number of cards, all cards are dealt out and there is no trump.
howToPlay.step3Description: Starting left of the dealer, each player predicts how many tricks they will win. The catch: the total predictions cannot equal the number of cards dealt.
howToPlay.step4Description: The player left of the dealer leads the first trick. Players must follow suit if they can. The highest card of the led suit wins, unless a trump card is played.
howToPlay.step5Description: After all tricks are played, compare predictions to results. Correct guesses earn bonus points, wrong guesses cost you.

roundStructure.title: Round structure
roundStructure.description: The number of cards dealt changes every round, creating a unique pyramid pattern. The top round (maximum cards) is played twice. With {playerCount} players the game goes:
roundStructure.maxCards: Max cards per player = 52 / number of players (rounded down)
roundStructure.noTrumpDescription: When playing the maximum number of cards (the top rounds), there is no trump suit. All cards remaining are dealt out, so there is no card left to flip. These rounds are played without trump, making them extra challenging.

scoring.title: Scoring
scoring.correctDescription: You predicted 3 and won 3? That's 10 + 3 x 2 = 16 points (with default 2 points per trick).
scoring.wrongDescription: You predicted 3 but won 1? That's -(2 x 2) = -4 points. The penalty grows with bigger misses.
scoring.strategyDescription: Predicting 0 and winning 0 tricks is a safe play worth 10 points. But correctly predicting higher numbers pays off more.

appFeatures.title: How the app helps
appFeatures.feature1Description: No more mental math or arguments. The app calculates scores instantly after each round.
appFeatures.feature2Description: The app knows which round you are on, how many cards to deal, and when the game ends.
appFeatures.feature3Description: See cumulative scores after every round so you always know who is in the lead.
appFeatures.feature4Description: Made a mistake? Go back and fix any round. Totals update automatically.
appFeatures.feature5Description: The app prevents invalid entries: predictions cannot equal the round number, and actual tricks must add up correctly.
appFeatures.feature6Description: Set how many points each correctly won trick is worth when you create the game.

cta.title: Ready to play?
cta.description: Create an account and start your first Oh Hell game in seconds.

seo.title: Klaverjas score tracking and rules | Game Scoreboard Manager
seo.description: Learn Klaverjas rules, understand roem and scoring, and keep every round organized with an online score tracker.

howToPlayKlaverjas.title: How to play Klaverjas
howToPlayKlaverjas.subtitle: A classic Dutch trick-taking card game played in teams of two, where trump cards change everything and roem can turn the tide.

overview.title: Game overview
overview.playersDescription: 4 players in 2 teams. Partners sit across from each other.
overview.deckDescription: Piquet deck: 32 cards (7 and up in each suit). No cards below 7.
overview.goalDescription: Score more points than the opposing team by winning tricks with high-value cards and bonus combinations.
overview.teamsDescription: Two teams of two. You play with the person sitting across from you.

howToPlay.title: How to play
howToPlay.step1Description: The dealer gives each player 8 cards from the 32-card piquet deck, usually dealt in groups of 3-2-3 or 4-4.
howToPlay.step2Description: A trump suit is chosen. In classic play, a card is turned face-up to propose the trump suit. Players then bid to play or pass. The team that plays must score more than half the points. In the Jack and Nine are the highest trump cards.
howToPlay.step3Description: The player left of the dealer leads the first trick. You must follow suit if you can. If you cannot follow suit, you must trump (rules differ by variant). The highest card wins the trick.
howToPlay.step4Description: After all 8 tricks, count the card points won by each team. Add any roem (bonus combinations) scored during play. The playing team must have more than half the total to avoid going nat (wet).
howToPlay.step5Description: A full match is 16 hands (4 deals per player), or play until one team reaches 1500 points. The team with the most points wins.

cardValues.title: Card values
cardValues.description: Cards have different point values depending on whether they are trump or not. In trump, the Jack (Jas) and Nine (Nel) become the two most powerful cards.
cardValues.totalDescription: Every hand has exactly 162 points: 62 from trump, 3 x 30 from non-trump suits, plus 10 for the last trick. The playing team needs more than half (including roem) to avoid going nat.

roem.title: Roem (bonus points)
roem.description: Roem are bonus points for specific card combinations played within a single trick. They count toward the total when determining if the playing team goes nat.
roem.fourOfKind: Four of a kind (10, J, Q, K, or A)
roem.stukNote: If stuk is part of a sequence, both bonuses stack. For example: a four-in-a-row that includes the King and Queen of trump scores 50 + 20 = 70 points. Sequences follow standard order: 7-8-9-10-J-Q-K-A.

variants.title: Amsterdams vs. Rotterdams
variants.description: The two main rule variants differ in what happens when you cannot follow suit and your partner is already winning the trick.
variants.amsterdamsDescription: You do not have to trump if your partner is already winning the trick. You can freely discard a card from another suit. This creates more room for teamwork and signaling between partners.
variants.rotterdamsDescription: You must always trump (or overtrump) when you cannot follow suit, even if your partner is winning. Only if you have no trump at all may you play another suit.
variants.comparisonTitle: When a non-trump suit is led and you cannot follow suit:
variants.keyDifferenceDescription: In Amsterdams, when your partner has the highest card in the trick and you cannot follow suit, you are free to discard any card. In Rotterdams, you must always trump regardless. This makes Amsterdams more team-oriented with more signaling opportunities.

scoring.title: Scoring
scoring.winDescription: The playing team (who chose trump) must score more than half of all points, including roem. With 162 base points and no roem, that means more than 81 points.
scoring.natDescription: If the playing team does not score more than half, they go nat: they get 0 points and the opposing team receives all 162 points plus all roem from both teams.
scoring.pitDescription: If the playing team wins all 8 tricks, they score 100 extra roem on top of the 162 points. If the opposing team wins all 8 tricks, the playing team goes nat.
scoring.lastTrickDescription: The team that wins the last trick receives 10 bonus points. These are already included in the 162 total.
scoring.strategyDescription: Roem counts toward the nat threshold. If the opposing team scores roem, you need even more card points to stay safe. Sometimes it is better not to report your own roem if it would help the opponent.

appFeatures.title: How the app helps
appFeatures.feature1Description: The app handles the nat logic automatically. Enter points and roem, and it figures out who wins the hand.
appFeatures.feature2Description: Track roem (fame) per team per round. The app accounts for roem when calculating whether a team goes nat.
appFeatures.feature3Description: See cumulative team scores after every hand so you always know who is ahead.
appFeatures.feature4Description: Made a mistake? Go back and fix any round. Totals update automatically.
appFeatures.feature5Description: Clear visual markers show which rounds had a pit or went nat, so you can see key moments at a glance.
appFeatures.feature6Description: Set up team names when you create the game. The scoreboard keeps everything organized per team.

cta.title: Ready to play?
cta.description: Create an account and start your first Klaverjas game in seconds.
